sentences.text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]
The new things that they published.
Every day, we went down the street, and we saw there were placards, announcements, what we supposed to do.
And of course, it was all mostly was for the Jews, the restrictions.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first one-- I don't remember chronologically what was first, but I think the very first one were the armbands.
In the beginning, we wore yellow armbands.
Had to be a certain width.
I think it was on the left arm.
That, I'm not sure about.
That was the yellow armbands.
That was while we were still in town before they sent us to the ghetto.
Then you were supposed to, when you, when you walked on the street, on the pavement, a German walked towards you, you had to get off the pavement.
You had to take off your hat.
You were not allowed to touch a German.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were continuous restrictions printed every day.
They were taking away the radios, people that had radios, furs, jewelry.
We were in the lucky position.
We didn't have anything, so we didn't have to deliver anything.
We never had a radio.
My mother didn't have any jewelry.
Well, she had a wedding ring, you know.
And I remember, she had another ring she used to wear on her finger.
Furs, we didn't have.
So there was nothing to deliver.
All these kind of appliances, electrical appliances, I think, had to be given up.
Telephones.
You weren't allowed to have a-- Jews weren't allowed to have telephones.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were time restrictions, you know.
There was a-- and from what-- I don't remember the hours when we were allowed.
In, in any case, this kept on changing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then came the announcement about the ghetto.
The Germans came into Lodz in September.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It, it couldn't have been very much later.
It was in the winter, because I remember the snow.
I remember when we left.
We had a restricted area that was in a, in a section of town called Baluty.
B-A-L-U-T-Y.
Baluty.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Predominantly very poor Jewish section of town.
Huge section.
It was like a whole city.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they told us how much time we have until we have to clear the town, and we're not allowed to get in.
And then they put the wires up and all that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This couldn't have been later than January, December, January.
December '39 and January 1940.
I'm not sure about the date, but it had to have been around that time because it was still winter.
In the beginning, yes.
The first few weeks, I think we went to school.
Right after, things started to normalize a little bit after the Germans came in.
But the classes weren't normal.
Most of the teachers were missing.
They left town.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because I remember, we were learning to write the Gothic German.
German was the first, one of the first lessons we had to learn that was in the curriculum.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was, I think, the number-one subject was German.
And this-- when I went into-- in 1939, I went into the last grade of elementary, seventh grade.
And this was already messed up, which in-- actually, I didn't properly finish seventh grade.
Then in the ghetto, we had school, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember how long we-- couldn't have been very long.
In the same building, in the same house where I used to go to school before the war-- it wasn't very far from where we lived.
Used to walk there all the time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the announcement came that we had to leave town, we had to leave our apartments, the only place we could go was to my maternal grandparents, because they lived in Baluty.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So apparently, it was decided between them that we're going to move in with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Things weren't very comfortable.
They had a larger apartment than ours.
Also one huge room which was divided.
And that's where they put-- in fact, there was another gentleman there after we moved in that my grandfather used to work for, the owner of that company, the factory where they printed the materials.
[NON-ENGLISH], I don't know.
He had a tiny, little beard, a white beard.
He must have been a very rich man before the war.
And he stayed with them because he had nowhere else to stay.
He was kicked out of his apartment in town.
I remember the trip.
We rented the horse and little wagon so we can put all our belongings.
And I remember the Germans filming it.
I remember the soldiers standing there in the brown uniforms, the NSKK-- what was the [NON-ENGLISH], something-- with cameras, with movie cameras, filming it while we were going.
And I was sitting high there on top of all the-- our furniture, whatever we could, we were allowed to take.
I don't remember that we, we were allowed to take everything or some parts of it.
I don't even think we took furniture because there would be no room in my grandparents' apartment to put it.
But all our belongings, clothes, everything.
And we drove up our street, Konstantynowska, to Baluty, to my grandparents' house.
I don't remember the arrival.
I just remember-- in my mind's eye-- I just remember sitting on top of the wagon on all that stuff.
And I remember the filming.
That, I remember.
I was even happy to look there.
I was hoping that they would see me on the camera.
Quite possible that that film exists somewhere in an archive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we lived with our grandparents for quite a while.
And this was quite an experience.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My grandfather, at that time, I remember him coming home crying one day.
I saw my grandfather cry.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans called him in the street, and they cut his beard.
And to him, this was the greatest tragedy, you know, religious Jew to have his beard cut.
He cried.
My grandfather cried.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And another tragedy befell this family.
He had a son, a younger son, my mother's youngest brother, Jochanan.
I think he-- he wasn't much older than me.
I was 12.
And he was, I think, 17.
But nevertheless, I called him uncle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Can I go back a little bit to before the war?
Just remembered an incident.
Like I said, my parents weren't religious.
And my grandmother sent him, this same son, she sent him to my mother for something.
I don't remember what.
And it was Erev Pesach.
It was the day before Passover.
He came into our apartment.
And my mother was kneading a, a dough for cookies.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you know, this is hametz.
This is the day before Passover.
The apartment was supposed to be clean already from everything that has to do with bread.
My mother nearly died from fright.
And she couldn't tell him, [NON-ENGLISH], don't tell our mother.
Apparently, he was smart enough not to tell her that, because this would have been-- this, my grandmother couldn't comprehend, even though she knew that her daughter was not religious.
But to bake cookies the day before Passover, this is worse than murder, for her.
Apparently, he didn't tell her because there was never anything in the family.
Well, this uncle of mine, [NON-ENGLISH], he was the apple of my grandfather's eye.
He was a gorgeous-looking guy, tall, the payots down to here.
I remember his fingers, long, beautiful fingers.
He went to yeshiva, and he was a tremendous scholar.
And that was called that tragic Thursday.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He went out some way.
They caught him.
And that was it.
They never saw him again.
They took him.
The Germans caught him in the street, just with another group of people.
They were sent away.
They never saw him again.
This broke my grandfather.
This was the greatest tragedy to him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And another incident, which has to do something with my parents not being religious-- we were there in April [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of 1941.
April 13 is my birthday.
I was 13 years old.
I was never prepared nor did I ever have a bar mitzvah, because my parents were not religious.
And I remember sitting at the table with my grandfather.
And he said to my father-- these words I'll remember if I live another 1,000 years-- he said to my father, Hershel, you know how much it hurts me that I lost [NON-ENGLISH], his son.
But I think it causes me greater pain, the fact that [NON-ENGLISH], that me-- he called me [NON-ENGLISH]-- will not have a bar mitzvah.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't remember my father's answer.
But of course, they were stubborn, radical socialists at that time.
So I didn't have a bar mitzvah.
That caused them a lot of pain.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we stayed with our grandparents, they used to sell bread rolls and bread.
I guess he took it from a bakery and distributed it to the neighbors so he made an extra few pennies to survive.
He smoked a lot, my grandfather.
And he had asthma.
And the doctor told him not to stop smoking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Even though this probably killed him, he said it doesn't make any difference.
This is the only pleasure he's got left in his life.
And I remember him smoking.
And he died very early while we were there.
I think we still, when we still stayed with them, he passed away.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yes.
Yes.
Because I remember my parents going to the funeral.
A funeral at that time was no big deal.
You just went there to bury them, and that's it.
And the cemetery, the huge cemetery, the Lodz Jewish cemetery, which was, I think, one of the largest in Europe, was on the territory of the ghetto.
It was also there.
In fact, it was in the same street where my grandparents lived, but way back further away.
They lived Gornicza number 40, I think.
We were there in 1987.
And the cemetery, if I remember correctly, was 156.
And this was a long way away.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] For me personally, maybe it wasn't as traumatic as for a grownup, being only 13 years old.
I found a lot of kids in the surrounding backyards that I got friendly with.
I didn't go to school then.
While we were with them there-- which means the beginning of 1940-- I didn't go into school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My paternal grandparents [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were taken away with an [NON-ENGLISH].
They were sent out of Lodz.
And one sister, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father's sister, Topcha, blonde, blue-eyed.
She looked like a shiksa.
She didn't look Jewish at all.
She could've saved herself if she would have gone away.
She was the only one left.
I think that she didn't sleep at home when they came for them.
She must have stayed with us.
She was very close with my father and with me.
She wasn't married.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Can I go back again?
When I came back from that camp in 1939, from the summer camp-- she loved me very much.
She waited for me at the bus station with a, with a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH] are?
One-horse carriage, you know.
It's like a taxi, but that was it.
She waited for me there.
She picked me up with it, actually brought me home, [NON-ENGLISH] Topcha.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Apparently, she stayed with us because she was left.
They sent away the whole family, the sister that lived at this house, another younger brother and-- my father had two younger brothers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wolf was the youngest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Herka-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [CRYING]
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK.
My cousins.
[CRYING]
So many.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And they don't believe it.
Where are they?
If it didn't happen, where are they all?
[CRYING] Thank you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They all lived in the same street with my grandparents very close to each other.
See, I didn't even think about it before.
The youngest uncle, [NON-ENGLISH], had one son.
They managed to have one child, [NON-ENGLISH].
All I remember, his black eyes.
All you saw was black, they were shining like coal.
He was maybe five years old when the war started.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] So my aunt [NON-ENGLISH] Topcha, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was the only one left when they sent them all away.
And when we got our apartment in the ghetto, she moved in with us, and she stayed with us in this one little room.
The room was, I think, smaller than this living room.
[SIGHS] We had a garden in the backyard.
I mean, every possible piece of soil was used up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We turned over the ground, and we received some seeds from the Jewish committee in the ghetto so that we could plant.
Oh, they have seeds for the red beets.
Potatoes, we couldn't get.
Because if we got potatoes, you know, seed potatoes, hot potatoes were-- we cooked and we ate them.
I think cucumbers in the beginning.
I think we had some tomato seeds also.
So we used up every possible piece of ground.
That's why gardening is left to mine.
In my life, I have a beautiful garden out on Hollywood Boulevard, a public garden, because of that.
Because the smell of the soil is still in me.
And we grew things for, I think, the first two or three years.
We used to get seeds with less and less.
And my aunt lived with us.
My father couldn't get a job in the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One time, I think-- I don't remember how they found out where his parents were.
And they went to the post office in the ghetto to send away a package from the meager food rations that we have.
It was either the post office or the police station.
I don't remember that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were waiting in line a whole day.
And, you know, all the [NON-ENGLISH], favoritism, people that they knew got in first.
There was-- and my father, being a revolutionary, couldn't stand it.
So apparently he started yelling at the police with a few other people, and they beat them up.
I remember-- he picked up a, a seat, was going to throw it at the police.
They beat them up.
I remember, he came home all bloody here on his head.
He, he was starting to lose his hair here.
So it was all bloody because of that.
And I think the police arrested him.
I'm talking about the Jewish police in the ghetto.
I think he stayed one night in jail or something.
They let him out.
And they couldn't send away the package anyway.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He couldn't get a job.
He couldn't work.
So there was no money.
There was not even any money to buy the meager rations that we got.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they asked for volunteers to go to a war camp in Poznan, which was another big city in Poland that was maybe 200 miles away.

Well, apparently there were plenty of discussions between him and my mother and my, and his sister about whether he should go or not because the, the announcement said that if they go away, if they go to that camp, they'll get food.
They'll get living quarters.
And the families will get money, will get the payments every two weeks.
So he volunteered, and he went away.
May 1, 1941 was the last time I ever saw him.
[SOBS] That was the day of their wedding anniversary.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were married May 1, 1926.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We used to get letters from him from the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the beginning, they even sent a photo, him and a few people that worked with him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And, and then the letters stopped.
But we used to get the checks for quite a while, every two weeks.
I don't remember what it was.
Probably just enough to buy the food rations.
And this, with our logic, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we assumed that because they're still paying us, that he is alive, that he's working.
Otherwise, why would they pay?
This was our logic.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] About my father, I am not sure.
I don't know what happened.
One time, about two or three years later, two years-- this was '41-- maybe in '42 or '43, they sent back some people from that camp.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In fact, a neighbor of ours-- [NON-ENGLISH], I think, or [NON-ENGLISH]-- he came back from that camp.
And he said that he worked with my father.
But he didn't know anything about it, whether he came back or not.
I remember standing in the street seeing the trucks bringing the people back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw a person sitting in the truck with a face smaller than my fist.
And I'm not sure.
Until today, I'm not sure whether it was my father or not.
At the moment, I thought maybe it was.
But this person couldn't live more than another hour something.
I'm not sure.
I'm still not sure, even now.
But I remember looking at this person.
The truck drove past us.
They took them into the Jewish jail in the ghetto.
[SIGHS] I don't know.
Because later on, I heard different rumors that he was hung there in, in Poznan.
So I'm not sure whether I saw him or-- I never told this to my mother.
And I'm still not sure.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lived with my aunt.
I don't remember what year this was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Different instructions came in every day, different rules, different laws.
And they kept on tearing away people from the families under different pretexts.
At one time, they needed people that were between 50 and 60, 60 and 70, people with one child, people without children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this particular time, they asked for people that were by themselves.
And my aunt, of course, had no proof that she wasn't.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So she went to the, with the transport.
I remember my mother helping her pack.
I remember saying goodbye to her.
I never said goodbye to my mother.
But to her, I said goodbye because she left from our apartment.
That's the one, that's my aunt that waited for me when I came back from the summer camp, [NON-ENGLISH] Topcha.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we never saw her again.
So I was left with my mother alone.
My mother worked in a factory being a seamstress, you know.
She knew, she knew how to use a machine.
So through some friends, she got that job in the factory also as a, working as a machinist.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody had to work, no matter how old you were.
I managed to get into a factory, to a fur factory.
One of my father's uncles was a-- two brothers, they were fur merchants.
[NON-ENGLISH], very rich people before the war.
His brother-- he had another brother that left for Palestine at that time, before the war.
And I remember the story they were telling about [NON-ENGLISH].
Her name was [NON-ENGLISH].
She drove him crazy.
She wanted to go to-- she said, I don't-- this was a constant saying in the family.
I kept repeating what she said.
I don't want my body to wander after I'm dead to Mother Rachel's tomb.
I want to be buried there.
So since he was a rich man, he could afford it.
They went to Palestine before the war, I think in 1936.
I remember I used to be scared of him.
He was a tall man with a long, brown beard.
And sure enough, six months after they arrived, she died, and she's buried near Mother Rachel's tomb.
So this other brother, [NON-ENGLISH], he worked at that factory as a-- he was, they were dyeing furs there.
And of course, everything, all the furs were done for the army, for the German army.
And through him, I got a job there in the fur, in that huge concern.
Hundreds of people worked there.
And that's where I learned the trade, the fur trade.
Learned to sew the machine by hand.
My mother taught me how to use a thimble.
That's why even now when I sew something, I have to use a thimble.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I worked at the fur factory.
I was very happy when I got the job there.
I remember coming home at evening, telling my mother about the day's work.
And I got my ration.
Otherwise, I don't think you were getting a ration.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was, I think, in the end of '41 or-- that I already worked in the, in the fur factory, [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember the, the president, the director of the factory, [NON-ENGLISH].
I met him after the war in Lodz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I worked there until the last minute, until they dissolved the ghetto and they sent us to Auschwitz.
The ghetto in Lodz was, in a sense, worse than Auschwitz.
When we came to Auschwitz, you knew you got your ration, your food.
In the ghetto, the only thing, we were still together with the families.
But the atmosphere in the ghetto-- you will hear about the ghetto from different people, different opinions, which depends on your character, the way you take things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were nice things I remember from the ghetto.
I remember how [INAUDIBLE] organization, the SKIF, made a party in June of 1942, the day when the Russians bombed Kiev, and they started the war against Russia.
We figured, this is it.
This is where our liberation is going to come from.
The war is going to end as soon as the Russians come in.
They going to come and liberate us.
Little did we know that they didn't give a hoot about us.
But this was our dream.
The Messiah is going to come only from there.
Comrade Stalin is going to save us.
We had a party.
I remember what we got.
I, I don't know where they got this.
Everybody got a slice of bread with honey.
That was the party to celebrate the fact that Soviet Russia went into the war against the Germans.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The-- what was the most traumatic thing in the ghetto was the continuous, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] practically every day, different publications of instructions on what we should do, who's going to be sent away.
And every day, there were different things.
One of the worst was a time-- and I don't remember what year.
I think it was '42.
It was called the [NON-ENGLISH], which is like the closing down.
They completely shut off the ghetto.
We weren't allowed-- we didn't go to work.
I don't remember-- it lasted quite a few days.
And the Germans just came into the homes and just ripped people out.
First, they took the children, young children.
And the president of the ghetto, Mordechai Rumkowski, he was the king, the god of the ghetto.
He came.
There was a huge meeting on the-- where was it?
The plaza of the, the fire brigade, the Jewish fire brigade.
Called everybody who went there.
And I remember the words he said.
And I still think for that alone, they should have burned him alive.
He was 77 years old, and he uttered the words, Jews, a terrible tragedy befell us.
Give up your children.
Of course, it was an order from the Germans.
But this is what he said.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And they took most of the children out.
Among them, my cousin, a girl, Marilke.
How old was she?
Five years old?
That was my mother's youngest brother.
In fact, he was named after the same great-grandfather.
That was Jakov.
